metadata.title: A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES: SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES*

abstract: Social movements scholars have widely used the framing perspective to analyze meaningmaking related to social movements and contentious politics. Qualitative methods have helped to illuminate how activists frame social issues to combine meanings in strategic ways. By contrast, linear statistical modeling is ill-suited to analyze the interdependent and circuitous aspects of collective action frames. This study offers a multimethod approach that uses an abductive framework to combine techniques from computational text analysis and network modeling along with interpretive coding. I demonstrate this approach in the context of framing disputes through legal mobilization over the same-sex marriage in the case of Obergefell v. Hodges. By examining Court discourse and amicus briefs, I show the coordination of similarities and distinctions among opposing social movement groups and elite actors. Future research can expand this method for both case studies and comparative analyses of movements.

paragraphs: Social movements are centrally concerned with changing the meanings of identities, actions, and social arrangements of power. By targeting the vocabularies and grammars of social action, movements can transform individuals and communities while also working to reconfigure the sociological imagination in ways that produce short-and long-term changes at the collective level (Armstrong 2002;Benford and Hunt 1992;Mohr, Wagner-Pacifici, Breiger, and Bogdanov 2013;Vicari 2010). Such movements rely on sustained collective action, which is itself a social construct centered around communication among participants and with powerful targets and publics (Melucci 1996). Within the study of social movements, the framing perspective has gained wide utility by building from the metaphor of cognitive frames to examine how collective actors deploy meanings to mobilize and sustain movement activity (Snow 2007;Snow, Rochford, Worden, and Benford 1986;Snow, Benford, McCammon, Hewitt, and Fitzgerald 2014). In this article, I demonstrate a multimethod approach to describe and analyze framing disputes. By investigating discourse contending over the legalization of same-sex marriage, I show how these tools can model a multiplicity of meanings deployed by opposing movement actors and elite institutions.

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: Collective action frames diagnose a social problem by reorienting individual and communitylevel tension onto social norms and structural arrangements of power (Benford 1993). Amidst a range of ever-present social problems, movements associate specific issues with possibilities for emergent collective action that disrupt the status quo in a meaningful way (Polletta 1998;Blee 2014). When successful, movements link these collective actions to a larger agenda of change that yields solutions to the underlying causes of social problems (Benford 1993). To explicate the dynamic and relational process of framing, I draw on the three-part rendering put forward by Leo d 'Anjou and John Van Male (1998). They analyze frames as interpretive packages that combine (1) culturally dominant values, (2) established oppositional themes, and (3) emergent topics that are rising in popularity. This definition of frames points to their relational formation and deployment in balancing tensions between interpreting the world as it is, establishing the potential for change, and motivating action toward new forms of social organization.

This dynamic conception is necessary in part because movements are always framing issues to multiple audiences at once. The successful reception of these types of claims depends on the multiple contexts in which audiences hear them (McCammon 2012;McCammon, Campbell, Granberg, and Mowery 2001;McCammon, Muse, Newman, and Terrell 2007). First, organizations and spokespeople represent their claims in ways that reflect the beliefs and values of their constituents and allies. Among supporters, frames align and affirm a sense of shared identity that can sustain collective action by a diverse group of actors unified through shared values, beliefs, and/or goals (Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011). This internal cohesion is accomplished through contrast with opposition to external targets (Taylor and Whittier 1992). Further, movements orient themselves to elite targets and powerful institutions across multiple institutional fields, making strategic claims spanning political, economic, and cultural domains (Armstrong and Bernstein 2008;Van Dyke and Taylor 2017).

While movements must necessarily distinguish themselves from powerful institutions to garner grassroots support, they also utilize strategic sponsorships and partnerships with elite allies (Tarrow 2011). Elite allies can be particularly useful in situations where movements are faced with countermobilization (Meyer and Staggenborg 1996). These types of opposing movement situations may be more common in contemporary society. New technologies have vastly reduced the cost for actions associated with social movement mobilization and spawned new forms of protest (Earl andKimport 2008, 2009;Earl, Kimport, Prieto, Rush, and Reynoso 2010). Thus, while each movement finds itself in a different configuration relative to its supporters and opponents, the general situation of movements includes up to four audiences: the movement's constituency, powerful opposed targets, elite allies, and countermovement activists and organizations. For this opposing movement arrangement, the role of emergent themes may be diminished, as established movements and countermovements tend to polarize around distinctions between "us" and "them" that override the use of new and emergent themes (Meyer and Staggenborg 1996). This has often been the case in same-sex marriage debates, both between liberals and conservatives, as well as among LGBT and queer communities (see Kimport 2013;Stein 2002;Weber 2015).

To help stabilize an analysis of dynamic, competing conversations, two concepts from social movement scholarship are particularly germane here: anchors and bridges (Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011;Reese, Gandy, and Grant 2003;Robnett 1997). Anchors establish boundaries onto a domain of movement, creating restrictions of scope that are necessary for discourse to become understandable. That is, anchors help consolidate disparate and competing meanings under powerful themes and values. Bridges, by contrast, link spheres of discourse that become necessarily distinct (Robnett 1997;Stovel and Shaw 2012). Belinda Robnett's (1997) concept of "bridge leadership" demonstrates this in the practice of women in the civil rights movements linking the concerns of grassroots constituents with movement organization's formal leaders and decision makers. In a more general sense, the linking of disparate groups, coalitions, and the work of "strange bedfellows" contributes to myriad movement campaigns and their various successes (Dorf and Tarrow 2014;Van Dyke and McCammon 2010). In a rhetorical sense, bridges help to expose the contradictions between opposed positions by creating something new. Relative to anchors, these bridges serve a complementary function by organizing ideas that refuse to unify or coalesce.

To operationalize these concepts, this study uses a multimethod framework to analyze framing disputes between opposing movements and elite decision makers. Examining how shared and competing meanings are framed for multiple audiences illuminates their dynamic placement in dominant, oppositional, and polarized positions. This is demonstrated through legal contention over the Supreme Court of the United States' (SCOTUS) ruling in Obergefell v. Hodges, which fully legalized same-sex marriage nationally. This method of study demonstrates how computationally assisted text analysis and network models can be used to represent, visualize, and interpret the relational organization of movement discourse. In doing so, this study joins a chorus of scholars that have called for greater attention to bridging the strengths of qualitative methods and big data analysis (Evans and Aceves 2016;Lazer and Radford 2017;Nelson 2017). I first draw from existing literature to show the nested dynamics of power and contention between opposing movements and divided elites in the context of legal mobilization. I then review these dynamics in the specific context of same-sex marriage law using amicus briefs submitted for the Obergefell case.

sections.header_raw: LEGAL FRAMING: COURT DISCOURSE AND AMICUS BRIEFS
sections.header_norm: legal framing court discourse and amicus briefs
paragraphs: The Supreme Court's position as an elite actor within a hierarchal state institution often distances it from the analytic tools used to examine grassroots social movements. By positioning both movements and institutions as sites of discursive contention, we can apply the theory of collective action frames to the dynamics of legal mobilization. Judicial opinions are structured by assembling precedent set in constitutional law with government practices as a process of constitutional review (Bricker 2019). These can be understood through the dynamics of framing as combinations of dominant, opposition, and emergent topics (d 'Anjou and Van Male 1998). First, the Court grounds its authority in the Constitution, which reflects culturally dominant understandings of U.S. governance. The contradictions embedded within the Constitution, including those made in its interpretation and amendments over time, have paved the way for long-standing oppositions that allow the Court to work at seemingly cross purposes with itself. These oppositions are contoured by ideological divisions reflected in the government officials who appoint and confirm justices. The enduring distinctions between liberal and conservative standpoints on the Court enable selective deployments of polarized arguments over time.

The Supreme Court's removed stance from the dynamics of electoral politics allows the performance of slow, deliberative work to interpret the state's fidelity to the Constitution. These tensions allow the Court to exist both as a safeguard for minority rights, as well as being limited by its own fidelity to authorizing the will of the majority (Rosenberg 2008;Scheingold 2004). Indeed, the authority of the Court depends on balancing these two positions. This balancing act is apparent through the public performances of the Court, including trials and announcing decisions. One particularly important public interaction for SCOTUS and social movements is the writing and submission of amicus ("friend of the court") briefs. These documents allow for a carefully limited type of engagement between the Court and the public.

Social movement organizations, individual activists, legal specialists, and political elites write and submit amicus briefs to influence the direction and form of the Court's decision on a particular case. Paul Collins (2004) describes two distinct pathways through which brief writers, or amici curiae, seek to influence the Court. First, brief signers signal the breadth of groups implicated in the Court's decision (Box-Steffensmeier, Christenson, and Hitt 2013). These groups can reflect potentially long-lasting and wide-reaching impacts of a legal decision. Second, briefs provide evidence that directly supports or refutes the cases' claimants. This informational pathway requires insider knowledge of legal and political argumentation, gained through specialized knowledge developed in professional practice (Collins 2004;Collins, Corley, and Hamner 2015) and through interpersonal intimacies (Lynch 2004). These pathways often co-exist within a case or even within a brief. This is exemplified in the routine practice of professionalized social movement organizations, who provide technical expertise and collaborate with groups from a range of industries depending on the specific context of a case (Box-Steffensmeier and Christenson 2014).

Although amicus briefs are a matter of course in SCOTUS cases, they are not always sites of opposing movement mobilization. Similarly, while the Court can deploy oppositional and polarized meanings, the justices can also remain in the realm of dominant frameworks, changing little outside of the scope of internal government discourse. But, in situations where the Court is meaningfully divided, a new set of opportunities becomes available for movements interested in advancing both short-and long-term legal interests. Further, when two or more movements converge over the outcome of a case, they form an arrangement of embedded, opposed contention, visualized through the example of samesex marriage in figure 1.

Further, when two or more movements converge over the outcome of a case, they form an arrangement of embedded, opposed contention, visualized through the example of samesex marriage in figure 1.

sections.header_raw: EMBEDDED, OPPOSING MOVEMENT SITUATIONS AND SAME-SEX MARRIAGE FRAMING
sections.header_norm: embedded opposing movement situations and same sex marriage framing
paragraphs: Same-sex marriage is a useful example for understanding the dynamics of meaning making deployed by opposing movements. Movements and countermovements have mobilized around this issue, and their advocacy reflects polarized understandings of collective selfhood among lesbian/gay couples and their allies compared to heteronormative communities (Kimport 2013;Stein 2002;Weber 2015). This is characteristic of opposing movement dynamics, in which collective identities and frames begin to stabilize over the terms of opposition, especially over time. Debates over same-sex marriage have mobilized mainstream activists who support, co-opt, and attack lesbian/gay couples in contending over the terms of social and legal citizenship (Ghaziani, Taylor, and Stone 2016;Hart-Brinson 2016). Further, the opposing movements that formed over same-sex marriage also indicate meaningful divisions among elites. Although marriage equality advocates have changed the law, the state remains ideologically divided over this issue, thus enabling an analysis of both elite supporters and opponents.

In the 1990s, advocates for the heterosexual family legally codified the definition of marriage as between "one man and one woman" through the federal "Defense of Marriage Act" (DOMA) and state-level DOMAs. This defensive effort built on anxiety over the stability of heterosexual families amidst the demands of a rising gay rights movement (Dorf and Tarrow 2014). Early responses to these campaigns focused on the material benefits and civil procedures of marriage, prompting states, employers, and local governments to experiment with domestic partnerships and civil unions (Pawelski, Perrin, Foy, Allen, Crawford, Del Monte, Kaufman, Klein, Smith, Springer, Tanner, and Vickers 2006;Raeburn 2004;Tarrow 2013). These policies undercut the entitlement aspect of "special rights" frames and linked same-sex marriage to broader concerns about civil rights (Pizmony-Levy and Ponce 2013; Price, Nir, and Cappella 2005). After limited successes, advocates pivoted away from framing marriage as a set of rights to the language of love and commitment in order to increase the salience of this issue outside of legal and political domains (Tarrow 2013).

Same-sex marriage advocacy benefitted from media narratives that represented lesbians and gay men in family formations bound by romantic love between couples as the foundation for organizing kinship (Hull 2006;Landau 2009;Moscowitz 2013). Amidst these deployments, the "separate but equal" distinction of civil unions and domestic partnerships became increasingly distasteful relative to public support of gay and lesbian couples as a minority group (Cox 2000). Further, the viability of civil unions to replace religious/civil marriages greatly diminished when conservatives advocated for these policies to stem the tide of support for marriage equality (Burke and Bernstein 2014). The effort to incorporate lesbian/gay relationships through legal marriage acknowledges and fortifies the state's social role in sanctioning marriage contracts. This requires working within the dominant norms of U.S. citizenship, including constitutional law.

Struggles over the legal status of gays and lesbians, including same-sex marriage, have been a platform through which ideologically opposed "liberal" and "communitarian" interpretive frames of the Constitution are deployed (Levine 1997: 40). In the liberal vision, the Courts have a responsibility to redress state-sanctioned harm produced by the explicit foreclosure of marriage to lesbians and gay men. For instance, the Massachusetts Supreme Court's 2003 decision in Goodrige v. Department of Public Health initiated this legal argument by overturning a state-level Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA). This paved the way for local officers and activists in California, New York, New Mexico, and Oregon to combine mass wedding and courthouse protest tactics to stage spectacular displays of public support for gay marriage (Taylor, Kimport, Van Dyke, and Andersen 2009). These early protests gained little policy traction. Policy change did not arrive until five years later, starting in 2008 and 2009, when the highest courts in Connecticut, California, and Iowa reversed their state-level DOMAs. In 2008, California voters endorsed Proposition 8, reversing the Court's reversal. This strengthened calls for judicial restraint, characterized by the communitarian view of the Court (Levine 1997). In this view, the Court's role is primarily to preserve moral consensus, social order, and thus, the status quo. This relies on a rhetorical fundamentalism that precludes judicial review as a vehicle for change.

As a result, starting in 2010, same-sex marriage advocates focused their state-level efforts on legislative, executive, and popular change, while judicial challenges shifted to the federal court system. The multiple challenges to California's Prop 8 and the federal DOMA culminated in SCOTUS hearing the 2012 case United States v. Windsor. In the 5-4 Windsor decision, Justices Kennedy, Breyer, Ginsburg, Sotomayor, and Kagan partially overturned the federal DOMA in order to recognize same-sex marriages in states where it had already been legalized. Importantly, the decision did not mandate recognition between states, allowing state-level DOMAs and state-level same-sex marriage to exist concurrently. The Windsor decision provided a body of evidence and interpretive framework that emboldened federal court judges to overturn additional state-level bans. Between 2012 and 2015, the combined efforts of federal courts and state governments overturned all but fifteen state-level DOMAs. The Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals (covering Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and Tennessee) emerged as the only federal court to uphold the state's interest in enforcing a strictly heterosexual definition of marriage.

In this context, the Supreme Court heard the 2015 case James Obergefell et al. v. Richard Hodges et al. The petitioners (Obergefell et al.) were same-sex couples and widows seeking legal recognition of their relationships in sixth circuit states. Respondents in this case (Hodges et al.) were the state-level government officers who issue and sanction marriage licenses in Michigan, Kentucky, Ohio, and Tennessee. SCOTUS summarized the case in terms of two interrelated questions, picking up where U.S. v. Windsor left off: "Does the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment require that states (1) must authorize marriage licenses to consenting adults regardless of gender?; and (2) must residents' states recognize same-sex marriages legally performed in other states?".

The majority opinion in Obergefell completed what the Court began in Windsor by seeking to quell the social reproduction of heterosexual privilege through marriage. For many observers of the Court, this was unsurprising given that the justices did not change between the Windsor and Obergefell decisions. The calculated pause between the two cases created an opportunity for the lower courts to build consensus, demonstrating judicial restraint as a productive process. In the Obergefell case, amicus briefs represented movement claims and the translation/transformation of a broad discourse around marriage equality into the legal context. These claims are made legally powerful through their efficacy in influencing the decision issued by the Court. Despite the legal, political, social, and cultural momentum of same-sex marriage advocates, conservative opposition mounted a substantial defense, suggesting that this issue has a high degree of salience for movement and political actors' collective identities and ideologies (Stein 2002).

In sum, this study examines the conversations between social movements and SCOTUS in response to the consolidation and hearing of Obergefell v. Hodges, including their broader implications for movement mobilization. As this brief history of same-sex marriage advocacy has demonstrated, meaning making is multifaceted. These debates have divided elites and mobilized opposing movements over polarized identities and accordant legal, ideological, and practical beliefs. This is characteristic of the embedded, opposed contention visualized in figure 1. In this study, I demonstrate a multimethod framework guided by an abductive approach (Tavory and Timmermans 2014), using computational text analysis to model the networked organization of meanings as anchors and bridges (Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011;Reese, Gandy, and Grant 2003;Robnett 1997) to identify dominant, oppositional, and polarized frames (d 'Anjou and Van Male 1998) among both social movement groups and legal decisionmakers. Regarding same-sex marriage, this analysis will demonstrate how ideas about lesbian and gay families were used to mobilize enduringly opposed narratives of state action to both legalize same-sex marriage and endorse religious liberty by consolidating the rights of private individuals within public institutions.

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: I analyzed the amicus briefs and Court discourse using a multimethod framework utilizing an abductive approach (Tavory and Timmermans 2014). As a theory of method, abduction combines the inductive logic of emergence with the deductive logic of structure (Tavory and Timmermans 2014;Timmermans and Tavory 2012). This necessitates the use of multiple methods, in order to evaluate the validity of competing "alternative casings" of any given data set (Timmermans and Tavory 2012). Leveraging such a methodological ambivalence sidesteps the dichotomy between testing or constructing theory and positions method as a conversation between theory and data.

All computational analyses were conducted using R, version 3.3.1. First, I used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling, an unsupervised machine learning procedure with an inductive logic to code the corpus of documents utilizing only the text presented therein (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003). The quantitative estimates produced through LDA topic modeling were then utilized to create networks of topics motivated by the co-occurrence of themes within documents. These network models were evaluated and refined iteratively by comparing the quantitative representations of the text with the written content itself, as well as existing theoretical accounts of framing disputes, samesex marriage, and legal advocacy. These comparisons allowed for the introduction and evaluation of multiple potential narrations of these conversations (Dye, Schatz, Rosenberg, and Coleman 2000;Tavory and Timmermans 2014), providing nuance and complexity associated with the techniques of close reading and thick description (Geertz 1994;Mohr, Wagner-Pacifici, and Breiger 2015;Ponterotto 2006). This approach uses a multimethod logic that goes beyond deploying quantitative and qualitative procedures to combining the sensibilities of both computational and interpretive approaches to social analysis.

paragraphs: In order to examine the dynamics of framing disputes among opposing movements as well as between movement claimants and decision makers relative to the Obergefell case, my analysis centers the Court's majority opinion decision, dissenting opinions, and the amicus briefs submitted in support of both sides (n = 150). The Court's decision included: (1) a syllabus document prepared by the staff of the Court to summarize and distill the implications of the decision for direct policymaking and press coverage; (2) an opening section acknowledging the historical background of marriage and the law; (3) the constitutional argument interpreting the Fourteenth Amendment to justify the legalization of same-sex marriage; and (4) a conclusion emphasizing the policy mandate leveled to dissenting members of state and federal government offices (n = 4). The decision was supported by a slim majority of the Supreme Court (5-4), and the dissenting justices strongly evidenced their opposition by each submitting a dissenting opinion, with Scalia and Thomas joining each other's opinions as well as with Roberts and Alito (n = 4).

The large and relatively even number of briefs submitted by both supporters and opponents of same-sex marriage enables an analysis of the competing logics put forth by both sides' arguments. In this case, individuals and organizations submitted 148 amicus briefs to influence the content and form of the Supreme Court's decision. Among these briefs, just over half were submitted in favor of the petitioners (i.e., the same-sex couples and widows seeking to have their relationships legally recognized, n = 75). Slightly fewer briefs were submitted by amicus curiae supporting the respondents (i.e., the state governments upholding an exclusively heterosexual definition of marriage, n = 67). Six additional briefs were submitted neutrally, focusing on procedural aspects of the case. These were excluded from the present analyses, given the focus on opposing movement dynamics. For the purposes of topic modeling, I treated the full text of each amicus brief's "Arguments" section as a separate document (n = 142).

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: I used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling to quantitatively represent the content of legal discourse around same-sex marriage that emerged from the Obergefell case. LDA topic modeling uses unsupervised machine learning to process a set of documents and group the vocabulary of words used into ranked lists, called "topics" (Blei et al., 2003;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013). Topic modeling has strong affinities with both a cultural theoretical framework (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013) as well as network-based approaches (Chang and Blei 2009). The machine learning procedure generates topics by iteratively grouping and ranking words by their frequency and patterns of proximity with other words. For instance, when the words "same", "sex" and "marriage" are used regularly and in conjunction with one another, the procedure groups these words and other words that regularly occur near them throughout the corpus of documents. In addition to weighting the words that indicate "topics" in the corpus, LDA estimates the relative presence of topics within each document as a probability distribution.

Topic modeling privileges the semantic process of word choice over the structure, tone, and flow of written content. That is, the procedure might group the terms "same", "sex", and "marriage" together, but it does not recognize the phrase "same sex marriage" as a distinct entity. This makes topic modeling well-suited for analysis of legal documents, such as Court opinions and amicus briefs. The norms of legal communication treat linguistic specificity as a site of power, ensuring that words are drawn from a limited vocabulary and used with specific meanings. As well, topic modeling has been deployed in studies of social movement frames in ways that align with the flexible application of this concept across multiple movement processes (Bail 2016;Kinney, Davis andZhang 2018, Light andCunningham 2016;Nelson 2015).

To increase the validity of results, I removed common English stop words, terms occurring fewer than three times across the entire corpus of documents. The number of topics in a given model is selected by the researcher and can be evaluated with respect to both content validity and statistical measures, such as perplexity and held-out likelihood (Chuang, Gupta, Manning, and Heer 2013). However, these measures do not always identify models that are correlated with a human reader's judgment of relevant and interpretable information (Chang, Boyd-Graber, Wang, Gerrish, and Blei 2009;Chuang, Ramage, Manning, and Heer 2012). For this study, I relied on testing the theoretical relevance and substantive validity of topic models with various numbers of topics for use in network models, as described below. Future research might consider testing how statistical measures correlates with validity for use in network models, building from the model presented here.

In order to effectively utilize this procedure to examine framing disputes between opposing social movements and elites contending over same-sex marriage, I compared topic models with different numbers of topics. First, I ran topic models for 5-30 topics with the amicus briefs and Court documents. The solutions for five to ten topics failed to distinguish between substantively meaningful aspects of the case by grouping competing ideas into a single topic. The models using twenty-six to thirty topics showed substantial repetition in the top-ranked keywords, arbitrarily splitting ideas over multiple topics. I thus selected the range of eleven to twenty-five topics for close investigation. I assigned preliminary names to each of these topics and reviewed the amicus briefs and Court documents most strongly associated with them. Next, I conducted preliminary analyses of the networks models described in the following section. I present the fifteen-topic solution as it captures a range of topics necessary to represent competing claims as well as the specificity sufficient to link them in a shared body of discourse. The assigned names for these topics, as well as the top keywords grouped by LDA are presented in table 1. In table 2, I summarize the estimated distributions of these topics over the groups of amicus briefs supporting and opposed to same-sex marriage, as well as the two stances of the Court in the Obergefell case.

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: Network models can show how connections between topics reflect framing disputes among brief signers and the Court. The contentious discourse over Obergefell was modeled as embedded conversations between opposing forces at the movement and elite levels. Thus, the documents were analyzed as four networks divided the documents by their position as movement or elite discourse (i.e., amicus briefs or Court documents) and their position on same-sex marriage (i.e., supporting or opposed). I modeled these networks by linking topics to each other based on their shared occurrence within documents. This mirrors the logic of topic modeling by estimating the multiple relationships between units of meaning relative to their shared proximity and extends further to estimate the relationships between actors based on their shared engagement over topics. In computational terms, the topic-bydocument distributions generated by LDA were treated as a two-mode adjacency matrix. They were then transformed into a one-mode network of topics by matrix-multiplying the topic-by-document by its transpose (Breiger 1974;see also, Opsahl 2013). This creates edge weights between topics from the sum of the pairwise products of topic weights.

These networks were first analyzed in terms of the distribution of centrality scores for topics within each group of documents: the Court's decision documents, the four dissenting opinions, amicus briefs supporting legal same-sex marriage, and amicus briefs opposing legal same-sex marriage. Two centrality measures were used: eigenvector centrality and betweenness centrality. Frames working in an "anchoring" position were identified using eigenvector centrality (Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011). To discern bridge topics, betweenness centrality was used. This network-statistical measure indicates those connections which "broker" between otherwise distinct groups of topics (Freeman 1977;Stovel and Shaw 2012). These scores are summarized in table 3a and 3b. I assessed these measures relatively. Topics were considered anchors if they had eigenvector centrality above the mean value for that network. Similarly, topics were considered bridges if they had betweenness centrality above the average score for that network. As these measures are somewhat correlated, multiple topics scored high on both measures of centrality. Topics that scored above average on both measures were considered anchors, as their betweenness score was influenced by their generally central position in the network. Bridges were then identified as topics with high betweenness centrality, but not eigenvector centrality.

Topic-based networks of document content are likely to be dense, given that the LDA procedure assigns non-zero probabilities to all topics for each document. That is, every document is considered to have at least some chance of including every single topic. For this analysis, topics estimated at less than 5% of a document were considered marginal and removed. It is likely that most of these topics are not actually present in a given document, or if present, they would only be given a very short consideration. Among the networks of topics based on the amicus briefs, the data were reduced by removing ties that weighted below the median edge-weight for that graph. Eigenvector centrality scores were nearly identical for the full and reduced networks. Betweenness centrality scores were markedly different between the full and reduced networks. Among the complete networks, betweenness centrality scores were inflated for the topics making up the smallest portion of the sub-group of documents. Their relatively small size, compounded over the sum of pairwise products, positioned them with numerous weak ties. To ascertain bridging topics that play a role in the manifest conversations among each group, only the reduced over-median networks were used. Topics scoring high on betweenness centrality in these networks were positioned in strategic relationships around anchoring topics, as well as varying in relative size over the corpus.

The networked relations of topics were analyzed iteratively, following the abductive logic of testing multiple explanatory models (Tavory and Timmermans 2014). Specifically, central topics were examined within groups and then across, while also checking and rechecking interpretations against the legal documents, as well as existing literature. Framing processes were analyzed by categorizing the centrality of topics in one or more networks concurrently. Specifically, topics were sorted into dominant, oppositional, and polarized themes by comparing the position of topics within and across the nested levels of legal discourse (d' Anjou and Van Male 1998). Dominant themes were identified as those topics that were highly central and in the same position across contending groups. For instance, when topics showed evidence of anchoring conversations among both briefs supporting and opposed to same-sex marriage, they are considered dominant anchors. Oppositional themes are those that were central across contending groups but in differing positions. For instance, a topic which anchored the dissenting opinions and scored as a bridge among the majority opinion is considered an oppositional topic. Finally, polarized themes were identified as topics that were distinctly central to one side of the conversation, either as an anchor or bridge. The interpretation of these topics was supplemented by reading the briefs and Court documents. Briefs were selected for closer examination based on their high loadings for one or more topics. The lead author of the top-2 briefs for each topic is shown in table 4 on the next page.

Topics that scored above average on both measures were considered anchors, as their betweenness score was influenced by their generally central position in the network. Bridges were then identified as topics with high betweenness centrality, but not eigenvector centrality.

Topic-based networks of document content are likely to be dense, given that the LDA procedure assigns non-zero probabilities to all topics for each document. That is, every document is considered to have at least some chance of including every single topic. For this analysis, topics estimated at less than 5% of a document were considered marginal and removed. It is likely that most of these topics are not actually present in a given document, or if present, they would only be given a very short consideration. Among the networks of topics based on the amicus briefs, the data were reduced by removing ties that weighted below the median edge-weight for that graph. Eigenvector centrality scores were nearly identical for the full and reduced networks. Betweenness centrality scores were markedly different between the full and reduced networks. Among the complete networks, betweenness centrality scores were inflated for the topics making up the smallest portion of the sub-group of documents. Their relatively small size, compounded over the sum of pairwise products, positioned them with numerous weak ties. To ascertain bridging topics that play a role in the manifest conversations among each group, only the reduced over-median networks were used. Topics scoring high on betweenness centrality in these networks were positioned in strategic relationships around anchoring topics, as well as varying in relative size over the corpus.

The networked relations of topics were analyzed iteratively, following the abductive logic of testing multiple explanatory models (Tavory and Timmermans 2014). Specifically, central topics were examined within groups and then across, while also checking and rechecking interpretations against the legal documents, as well as existing literature. Framing processes were analyzed by categorizing the centrality of topics in one or more networks concurrently. Specifically, topics were sorted into dominant, oppositional, and polarized themes by comparing the position of topics within and across the nested levels of legal discourse (d' Anjou and Van Male 1998). Dominant themes were identified as those topics that were highly central and in the same position across contending groups. For instance, when topics showed evidence of anchoring conversations among both briefs supporting and opposed to same-sex marriage, they are considered dominant anchors. Oppositional themes are those that were central across contending groups but in differing positions. For instance, a topic which anchored the dissenting opinions and scored as a bridge among the majority opinion is considered an oppositional topic. Finally, polarized themes were identified as topics that were distinctly central to one side of the conversation, either as an anchor or bridge. The interpretation of these topics was supplemented by reading the briefs and Court documents. Briefs were selected for closer examination based on their high loadings for one or more topics. The lead author of the top-2 briefs for each topic is shown in table 4 on the next page.

Network models can show how connections between topics reflect framing disputes among brief signers and the Court. The contentious discourse over Obergefell was modeled as embedded conversations between opposing forces at the movement and elite levels. Thus, the documents were analyzed as four networks divided the documents by their position as movement or elite discourse (i.e., amicus briefs or Court documents) and their position on same-sex marriage (i.e., supporting or opposed). I modeled these networks by linking topics to each other based on their shared occurrence within documents. This mirrors the logic of topic modeling by estimating the multiple relationships between units of meaning relative to their shared proximity and extends further to estimate the relationships between actors based on their shared engagement over topics. In computational terms, the topic-bydocument distributions generated by LDA were treated as a two-mode adjacency matrix. They were then transformed into a one-mode network of topics by matrix-multiplying the topic-by-document by its transpose (Breiger 1974;see also, Opsahl 2013). This creates edge weights between topics from the sum of the pairwise products of topic weights.

These networks were first analyzed in terms of the distribution of centrality scores for topics within each group of documents: the Court's decision documents, the four dissenting opinions, amicus briefs supporting legal same-sex marriage, and amicus briefs opposing legal same-sex marriage. Two centrality measures were used: eigenvector centrality and betweenness centrality. Frames working in an "anchoring" position were identified using eigenvector centrality (Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011). To discern bridge topics, betweenness centrality was used. This network-statistical measure indicates those connections which "broker" between otherwise distinct groups of topics (Freeman 1977;Stovel and Shaw 2012). These scores are summarized in table 3a and 3b. I assessed these measures relatively. Topics were considered anchors if they had eigenvector centrality above the mean value for that network. Similarly, topics were considered bridges if they had betweenness centrality above the average score for that network. As these measures are somewhat correlated, multiple topics scored high on both measures of centrality. Topics that scored above average on both measures were considered anchors, as their betweenness score was influenced by their generally central position in the network. Bridges were then identified as topics with high betweenness centrality, but not eigenvector centrality.

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: The networks of topics formed among the Court's discourse in the Obergefell case are visualized in figure 2. Seven topics were central to one or both sides of the Court discourse, as listed in table 5. Of these, three topics operated as dominant anchors, with high eigenvector centrality among both the decision documents and dissenting opinions. These are: (1) discussion of the Fourteenth Amendment; (2) concerns over the authority of the federal judiciary; and (3) the scope of fundamental civil rights. These shared anchors show the dominant understandings that form common ground between actors and limit the outer boundaries of legible contention. Notably, these three topics reflect the concerns summarized by the Court in hearing this case: whether the Fourteenth Amendment requires states to recognize marriages as a gender-neutral partnership and/or to recognize a same-

Table 5. Framing Processes for Central Topics in Obergefell Court Discourse Table 4. Lead Party for Top-2 Amicus Briefs to Framing Disputes sex marriage lawfully performed in another state. This supports the validity of using network graphs based on topic co-occurrence to model the framing processes of political actors. The Court set a particular scope in hearing the Obergefell case, which then sets the potential for stability and changes in the substantive discourse of the decision, as well as dissenting opinions.

Through discussion of the Fourteenth Amendment, SCOTUS elaborated the constitutional concepts of substantive due process and equal protection under the law. Particularly in the decision, the Fourteenth Amendment was positioned as a vehicle for protecting fundamental rights as sanctioned by the equal protection clause. This reconstruction-era amendment extended the constitutional concept of substantive due process established in the Fifth Amendment. Due process refers to the government's basic commitment not to harm its own citizens. Equal protection, then, legitimates the federal government's authority over states in enforcing this norm. In the Obergefell decision, SCOTUS argued that the Fourteenth Amendment requires all state governments to legally recognize marriages between samesex couples. This goes beyond the precedent set in Windsor, which established the federal definition of marriage as gender neutral, to mandating that state and local governments administer legal marriages between lesbian/gay couples. This interpretation of the Fourteenth Amendment mirrors the decision issued by the Court in the 1967 case Loving v. Virginia, which overturned bans on interracial marriage. The dissenting opinions do not dispute the interpretation of equal protection as set forth in Loving, but they do object to its application here. For the conservative justices, equal protection does enable the Court to protect preexisting rights, but in this situation, the right to marriage is only pre-existing for heterosexual couples (even those of different racial backgrounds) and is being artificially expanded by an overreaching judiciary action.

The Court's concerns with these limitations of the Fourteenth Amendment is apparent in the two additional topics which were positioned as dominant anchors: federaljudicial authority and the scope of fundamental civil rights. These contentions further Running Head: A Multi-Method Approach to Framing Disputes Notes. Topic names in bold had eigenvector centrality above the mean and are classified as anchors; topics in italics have betweenness centrality above the mean and are classified as bridges. See Table 2 for centrality scores. Heightened scrutiny Federal recognition 14 th Amendment Dependent benefits Federal authority Sexual subjects Everyday practices of marriage & family Public support Civil rights 14 th Amendment Federal authority Civil rights Federal recognition Outcomes of same-sex parenting Religious liberty Procreation Decision Dissents Notes: Topic names in bold had eigenvector centrality above the mean and are classified as anchors; topics in italics have betweenness centrality above the mean and are classified as bridges. See table 2 for centrality scores. operationalize the abstract constitutional concepts of due process and equal protection.

First, the absolute authority of the federal judiciary over legislative, executive, and popular political action (at both the state and federal levels) must continually be legitimated by its reserved deployment. With respect to protecting civil rights, this balancing act depends on defining a narrow scope of dignities that constitute fundamental rights. For the dissenting justices, the Court legalizing same-sex marriage exceeds its own power by authoring a new right to marriage for same-sex partners. By contrast, the decision asserts that marriage itself is a fundamental right, even though it has traditionally been defined in heterosexual terms. By positioning lesbians and gay men as citizens denied a right, the Court justifies its action as recognizing an already inherent right. The role of tension here demonstrates the importance of understanding dominant ideologies in relationship with ongoing opposition.

Although the opposed justices largely agree on the relationship between the power of the Court and the protection of civil rights, they maintain their differences by focusing on slight rhetorical distinctions with widespread practical implications. Specifically, the majority opinion counters the reservations of judicial authority by focusing on the processes of recognition that are central to the federalist project of the U.S. state. The topic of federal recognition operated centrally to both the decision and dissent networks, but in differing positions. It thus served as an oppositional frame between the two stances on same-sex marriage among the Court. Among the decision documents, federal recognition functioned as a fourth anchor. Discussion of this topic enabled the agency of the Court within its own limitations by connecting the three dominant anchors (Fourteenth Amendment, fundamental civil rights, and federal-judicial authority) with three topics that served as bridges: dependent benefits, heightened scrutiny, and public support. Because these topics were not central among the dissenting opinions, I consider them as "polarized bridges." These topics showed evidence of high betweenness centrality, due to their position in connecting constitutional concepts together with topics discussing the individual and community contexts of married same-sex couples (i.e., sexual subjects and everyday practices of love and family).

Dependent benefits and heightened scrutiny establish the boundaries of same-sex marriage as a civil right. First, discussion of dependent benefits shows how vital resources for spouses and families are distributed through legal marriages. These are channeled through economic exchange and employment, which are regulated through enduring social and cultural norms. Second, the arguments for heightened scrutiny relative to same-sex marriage contrast with the widely used notion of rational basis, which was deployed in the Windsor case. Rational basis is the most "commonsense" form of judicial review, in which laws can be overturned or revised by their conflicting position to the state's rhetorical and practical self-interests. This aligns with both due process and equal protection. Heightened scrutiny contrasts with rational basis in that it requires a legible category of subjects who have faced historically documented, state-sanctioned discrimination and are therefore able to access the state's attention in the ongoing maintenance and reproduction of the law (Hunter 2004). In this case, the Court implicitly refuted sexual orientation as a category of heightened scrutiny, legalizing only a right to gender-neutral marriage, not a fundamental right to be gay or lesbian. Under this carefully limited understanding of same-sex marriage, the Court drew from a range of evidence showing public support across legislative and public arenas, allowing them to recognize legal marriage equality as simply accelerating an already impending development.

First, the Court represented majority public support by reference to public opinion research that showed statistical estimates of greater than fifty percent approval for samesex marriage among American adults. These studies projected continually increasing support for same-sex marriage by associating opposition to the biased beliefs of older white Evangelical Protestants. This support is emboldened by the political will demonstrated by the existence of a widespread pro-gay constituency, including Democratic political leadership, in sixth-circuit states as well as similar Midwestern states where same-sex marriage was already legal (e.g., Minnesota). Told in this way, federal recognition, rather than judicial authority, allows the Court to observe and anticipate the groundswell of social, political, and cultural change around same-sex marriage in order to mandate states to legalize same-sex marriage.

The dissenting opinions also discuss processes of federal recognition, but it functions as a bridge, connecting the core discussion of constitutional concepts to secondary topics related to procreation and outcomes of same-sex parenting. Among this network, the topic of federal recognition showed evidence of high betweenness centrality, but not high eigenvector centrality. The dissenting justices called into question the definitional power of government actors and lower courts over marriage by emphasizing the role of procreation. In this account, the legitimacy of families is derived from their relationship with heterosexual reproduction. When naturalized as a historical fact, the heterosexual definition of family retains privilege over the seemingly short-term shifts in local and state-level policies. Further, the justices object to research findings showing evidence of positive outcomes for children raised by same-sex parents. They characterize these findings as both politically biased and insufficiently replicated. By sowing uncertainty in this way, the justices cling to the passive stance of the Court, threatening the potential for future embarrassment if same-sex marriage falls out of public and political favor. The perspectives of the political leaders, researchers, and everyday people implicated in these discussions can be more deeply understood by analyzing how these and additional topics were framed by amicus brief writers.

For the conservative justices, equal protection does enable the Court to protect preexisting rights, but in this situation, the right to marriage is only pre-existing for heterosexual couples (even those of different racial backgrounds) and is being artificially expanded by an overreaching judiciary action.

The Court's concerns with these limitations of the Fourteenth Amendment is apparent in the two additional topics which were positioned as dominant anchors: federaljudicial authority and the scope of fundamental civil rights. First, the absolute authority of the federal judiciary over legislative, executive, and popular political action (at both the state and federal levels) must continually be legitimated by its reserved deployment. With respect to protecting civil rights, this balancing act depends on defining a narrow scope of dignities that constitute fundamental rights. For the dissenting justices, the Court legalizing same-sex marriage exceeds its own power by authoring a new right to marriage for same-sex partners. By contrast, the decision asserts that marriage itself is a fundamental right, even though it has traditionally been defined in heterosexual terms. By positioning lesbians and gay men as citizens denied a right, the Court justifies its action as recognizing an already inherent right. The role of tension here demonstrates the importance of understanding dominant ideologies in relationship with ongoing opposition.

Although the opposed justices largely agree on the relationship between the power of the Court and the protection of civil rights, they maintain their differences by focusing on slight rhetorical distinctions with widespread practical implications. Specifically, the majority opinion counters the reservations of judicial authority by focusing on the processes of recognition that are central to the federalist project of the U.S. state. The topic of federal recognition operated centrally to both the decision and dissent networks, but in differing positions. It thus served as an oppositional frame between the two stances on same-sex marriage among the Court. Among the decision documents, federal recognition functioned as a fourth anchor. Discussion of this topic enabled the agency of the Court within its own limitations by connecting the three dominant anchors (Fourteenth Amendment, fundamental civil rights, and federal-judicial authority) with three topics that served as bridges: dependent benefits, heightened scrutiny, and public support. Because these topics were not central among the dissenting opinions, I consider them as "polarized bridges." These topics showed evidence of high betweenness centrality, due to their position in connecting constitutional concepts together with topics discussing the individual and community contexts of married same-sex couples (i.e., sexual subjects and everyday practices of love and family).

Dependent benefits and heightened scrutiny establish the boundaries of same-sex marriage as a civil right. First, discussion of dependent benefits shows how vital resources for spouses and families are distributed through legal marriages. These are channeled through economic exchange and employment, which are regulated through enduring social and cultural norms. Second, the arguments for heightened scrutiny relative to same-sex marriage contrast with the widely used notion of rational basis, which was deployed in the Windsor case. Rational basis is the most "commonsense" form of judicial review, in which laws can be overturned or revised by their conflicting position to the state's rhetorical and practical self-interests. This aligns with both due process and equal protection. Heightened scrutiny contrasts with rational basis in that it requires a legible category of subjects who have faced historically documented, state-sanctioned discrimination and are therefore able to access the state's attention in the ongoing maintenance and reproduction of the law (Hunter 2004). In this case, the Court implicitly refuted sexual orientation as a category of heightened scrutiny, legalizing only a right to gender-neutral marriage, not a fundamental right to be gay or lesbian. Under this carefully limited understanding of same-sex marriage, the Court drew from a range of evidence showing public support across legislative and public arenas, allowing them to recognize legal marriage equality as simply accelerating an already impending development.

First, the Court represented majority public support by reference to public opinion research that showed statistical estimates of greater than fifty percent approval for samesex marriage among American adults. These studies projected continually increasing support for same-sex marriage by associating opposition to the biased beliefs of older white Evangelical Protestants. This support is emboldened by the political will demonstrated by the existence of a widespread pro-gay constituency, including Democratic political leadership, in sixth-circuit states as well as similar Midwestern states where same-sex marriage was already legal (e.g., Minnesota). Told in this way, federal recognition, rather than judicial authority, allows the Court to observe and anticipate the groundswell of social, political, and cultural change around same-sex marriage in order to mandate states to legalize same-sex marriage.

The dissenting opinions also discuss processes of federal recognition, but it functions as a bridge, connecting the core discussion of constitutional concepts to secondary topics related to procreation and outcomes of same-sex parenting. Among this network, the topic of federal recognition showed evidence of high betweenness centrality, but not high eigenvector centrality. The dissenting justices called into question the definitional power of government actors and lower courts over marriage by emphasizing the role of procreation. In this account, the legitimacy of families is derived from their relationship with heterosexual reproduction. When naturalized as a historical fact, the heterosexual definition of family retains privilege over the seemingly short-term shifts in local and state-level policies. Further, the justices object to research findings showing evidence of positive outcomes for children raised by same-sex parents. They characterize these findings as both politically biased and insufficiently replicated. By sowing uncertainty in this way, the justices cling to the passive stance of the Court, threatening the potential for future embarrassment if same-sex marriage falls out of public and political favor. The perspectives of the political leaders, researchers, and everyday people implicated in these discussions can be more deeply understood by analyzing how these and additional topics were framed by amicus brief writers.

paragraphs: The amicus briefs largely work within the confines established by the Court as well as the norms of legal argumentation. This can be seen in the largely shared vocabulary deployed between the Court discourse and amicus briefs, which shared over 99% of their unique terms over the two sets of documents. This is somewhat unsurprising, as those who are highly critical of the state's definition of marriage are unlikely to spend their time and money preparing and submitting an amicus brief. While legal contention represents a domain of stability for the state, it also reflects processes of emergence and change that can introduce, elevate, and diminish sociolegal meanings that correspond to practical opportunities for movement advocacy. As shown in table 6, the greater breadth of discussion evident in the amicus briefs is shown through the wider range of topics. As well, topics that were shared across both sets of documents were deployed in distinct framing positions among the amicus briefs compared to Court discourse. For instance, federal recognition served as an oppositional theme among the Court, anchoring the majority opinion's justification for taking action, while bridging the dissenting justice's recalcitrant opposition to continued uncertainty. Among the briefs, it served as a dominant anchor by operating centrally to both opposing group's arguments. In addition, rational basis served as a second dominant anchor, scoring high on eigenvector centrality among both sets of amicus briefs.

The shared centrality of federal recognition and rational basis among the briefs shows a basis of common ground between ideologically divided groups on the importance of bottom-up approaches to policymaking. These groups, though, are divided by their arguments over who and how these policies should benefit individuals and communities. Specifically, two topics operated as oppositional themes among the briefs that were dominant anchors among the Court's discourse: fundamental civil rights and the Fourteenth Amendment. Although the Court shares a framework for how to manage fundamental civil rights and their enforcement through the Fourteenth Amendment, the amicus brief writers position these topics to maintain tension in how to interpret and apply these notions to everyday life. As well, a discussion of family formations was central to briefs on both sides as a third oppositional theme. This topic was not present among the Court's discourse. Rather, the distinct visions of healthy families advocated by amicus curiae center the practical debates that fuel high-level legal distinctions. Although these visions of healthy families are polarized, they also share the idea that families protect the private rights of individuals against the hostility of social life.

sections.header_raw: Pro-SSM Briefs and the Winning Narrative of Obergefell
sections.header_norm: pro ssm briefs and the winning narrative of obergefell
paragraphs: The discussion of family formations served as an oppositional anchor among the briefs supportive of same-sex marriage, with evidence of high eigenvector centrality in this network. This discussion centers families headed by same-sex couples amidst a range of family formations on the fringes of "traditional" nuclear families, including adoptive parents and step-parents. The networked positions of these topics are visualized in figure 3. Brief writers advocated for these families by arguing that legal recognition of samesex couples' relationships is a fundamental dignity. These briefs link this to the state's self-interest in legitimating these relationships as a matter of rational basis. Further, the fundamental dignity of couples is fortified within the interests of the state by virtue of lesbians' and gay men's participation in the military, federal government, and national economy. This concern justifies the expansion of family rights under the Fourteenth Amendment. This topic served as a bridge, connecting the anchoring topics with a polarized anchor: dependent benefits. The discussion of dependent benefits demonstrates families as sites of economic self-regulation through negotiated dynamics of kinship and attachment. For instance, the AFL-CIO and colleagues' brief demonstrates the productive labor of lesbians and gay men in private-and public-sector arenas. This topic underscores how the rights granted through marriage, including health insurance, tax benefits, and rights to children are already concordant with the incentives of free-market capitalism. Dependent benefits also linked these topics with a polarized anchor, heightened scrutiny, and a polarized bridge, military-economic citizenship. These explicate the justification for expanding family rights based on the recognition of new types of citizens, as well as the connection between opportunities to family life with federal authority across state lines. Arguments for heightened scrutiny were used to scaffold the calls for marriage equality under rational basis review. Both rational basis and heightened scrutiny arguments draw on bodies of evidence that position heterosexual marriage laws as state-sanctioned animus contraposed to the loving commitment of same-sex families. The groups advocating for heightened scrutiny showed that lesbians and gay men were categorized within and by the state as homosexuals for purposes of restricting access to citizenship opportunities. They go on by arguing that the existence of gay and lesbian orientations are immutable categories based on biogenetic evidence of diverse sexual orientations across populations and time. Although heightened scrutiny was neither used nor expanded through the Obergefell case, these arguments provide a motivation by which the existing state-level DOMAs are overturned. It is the once hidden existence of gay and lesbian citizens as healthy, functioning, members of normative institutions that situates lesbians and gay men as comfortable insiders to the project of national citizenship. Amicus curiae framed families headed by lesbian/gay couples as not only a legible element of national life, but as productive workers, soldiers, and caregivers--as normatively good citizens (Becker 2002;Brandzel 2005;Josephson 2005;Montegary 2015). The arguments extend beyond the social good of providing for these specific families to link these concerns with economic and military security. Notes. Topic names in bold had eigenvector centrality above the mean and are classified as anchors; topics in italics have betweenness centrality above the mean and are classified as bridges. See table 2 for centrality scores. Procreation Outcomes of samesex parenting Everyday practices of marriage & family Sexual subjects Family formations Heightened scrutiny Religious liberty Rational basis 14 th Amendment Federal authority Military-economic citizenship Dependent benefits Public support Federal recognition Civil rights Figure 3. Topic Network for Amicus Briefs Supporting Same-Sex Marriage in Obergefell

Same-sex families involved in large corporations, military service, and federal employment serve as exemplars for the terms of citizenship, while also highlighting the importance of mobility across state lines to underscore the need of a nationally unified definition of marriage. By describing families headed by same-sex couples as embedded within the normative flows of social and economic life, the briefs support a liberal vision of the state. This narrates the localized systems of support and surveillance that mitigate the behaviors, expectations, and opportunities of same-sex couples, especially in terms of their impact on children. Such a victorious narration of the movement for same-sex marriage centers same-sex couples, especially with children, who are positioned as naturally occurring by their status as prior to the state, with simultaneous reference to historical state violence waged through designations of homosexuality. The resilience of same-sex families is used to prove their authenticity as a form of social organization that can protect children from the hostile reactions of homophobic communities, laws, and institutions.

They go on by arguing that the existence of gay and lesbian orientations are immutable categories based on biogenetic evidence of diverse sexual orientations across populations and time. Although heightened scrutiny was neither used nor expanded through the Obergefell case, these arguments provide a motivation by which the existing state-level DOMAs are overturned. It is the once hidden existence of gay and lesbian citizens as healthy, functioning, members of normative institutions that situates lesbians and gay men as comfortable insiders to the project of national citizenship. Amicus curiae framed families headed by lesbian/gay couples as not only a legible element of national life, but as productive workers, soldiers, and caregivers--as normatively good citizens (Becker 2002;Brandzel 2005;Josephson 2005;Montegary 2015). The arguments extend beyond the social good of providing for these specific families to link these concerns with economic and military security. Notes. Topic names in bold had eigenvector centrality above the mean and are classified as anchors; topics in italics have betweenness centrality above the mean and are classified as bridges. See table 2 for centrality scores. Procreation Outcomes of samesex parenting Everyday practices of marriage & family Sexual subjects Family formations Heightened scrutiny Religious liberty Rational basis 14 th Amendment Federal authority Military-economic citizenship Dependent benefits Public support Federal recognition Civil rights Figure 3. Topic Network for Amicus Briefs Supporting Same-Sex Marriage in Obergefell

Same-sex families involved in large corporations, military service, and federal employment serve as exemplars for the terms of citizenship, while also highlighting the importance of mobility across state lines to underscore the need of a nationally unified definition of marriage. By describing families headed by same-sex couples as embedded within the normative flows of social and economic life, the briefs support a liberal vision of the state. This narrates the localized systems of support and surveillance that mitigate the behaviors, expectations, and opportunities of same-sex couples, especially in terms of their impact on children. Such a victorious narration of the movement for same-sex marriage centers same-sex couples, especially with children, who are positioned as naturally occurring by their status as prior to the state, with simultaneous reference to historical state violence waged through designations of homosexuality. The resilience of same-sex families is used to prove their authenticity as a form of social organization that can protect children from the hostile reactions of homophobic communities, laws, and institutions.

This discussion centers families headed by same-sex couples amidst a range of family formations on the fringes of "traditional" nuclear families, including adoptive parents and step-parents. The networked positions of these topics are visualized in figure 3.

sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
sections.header_raw: Conservative Responses to a New Status Quo: Family as Restraint, Social Control
sections.header_norm: conservative responses to a new status quo family as restraint social control
paragraphs: Briefs supporting the respondents included religious groups and government actors who accepted same-sex marriage as the new status quo and objected to its status as such by framing their arguments in terms of the private interests of individuals and institutions that feel threatened by the political enforcement of same-sex marriage. The arguments advanced by amicus brief signers opposed to same-sex marriage largely split between political and religious contentions. These separations entrench opposition among localized durable conflicts in legal, political, and scientific domains. As shown in figure 4, these topics combined a constellation of topics about state power in discussion of two dominant anchors: rational basis and federal recognition, as well as an oppositional anchor: the Fourteenth Amendment.

Those briefs that focused on the legal-political aspects of same-sex marriage primarily called for judicial restraint through discussion of federal-judicial authority, a polarized anchor. Amici arguing against legalization on procedural grounds emphasized the legalpolitical processes of judicial review and federal authority over the states invoked by the Fourteenth Amendment. These groups focused on states' rights to define marriage in heterosexual terms, but they were also careful to avoid using explicitly antigay rhetoric that fuel claims of animus, which can in turn escalate the need for heightened scrutiny in considering legal issues related to sexual orientation.

Brief signers shored up support for the state's interest in promoting heterosexuality by privileging the role of biological procreation in the social reproduction of gendered subjects. Procreation operated as a polarized anchor among these briefs. Specifically, amicus curiae argued that men and women provide distinct contributions to children's development, regardless of shared genetic material. For instance, "Organizations and Scholars of Gender-Diverse Parenting" and "Organizations that Promote Biologic Parenting" reported research findings showing that men and women provide unique and necessary contributions to children's healthy development. These groups privilege biological procreation by positioning marriage as an intentional process of self-regulation that acknowledges the nuclear family as an ideal against which reality can be measured. This works to couch religious beliefs about parents, children, and families in scientific and political terms and sidesteps the moral tensions between opposing groups of brief writers, mirroring the strategy used by advocates for same-sex marriage. In the conservative framework, the social, and political benefits of marriage are an incentive in exchange for the processes of self-denial implicit in family life. For instance, a group of amici named "Same-Sex Attracted Men and their Wives" submitted a brief that exemplifies this perspective. These individuals acknowledged and claimed their experiences of sexual and emotional desire for men but live and raise children with women in hopes of more effectively contributing to the stability and prosperity of their communities.

The potential threats of same-sex marriage were framed in terms of the oppositional theme, fundamental civil rights. While this operated as an anchor for pro-same-sexmarriage briefs, among those opposed, it functioned as a bridge. Notably, this topic cooccurred with the polarized bridge, religious liberty and freedom. Many briefs opposed to same-sex marriage used the equal protection frame that set the terms of the Obergefell case, but they argued that if the Court were to intervene in the matter of same-sex marriage, the proper action would be to overturn same-sex marriage laws and restore DOMAs. These arguments framed religious practitioners as aggrieved minorities whose rights were being threatened by the state, thus implicitly positioning same-sex marriage as already accomplished. The stakes of these individual conflicts were made salient by scaling up this conflict as one between state and religious organizations. For instance, the International College of Evangelical Endorsers submitted a brief arguing that military chaplains opposed to same-sex marriage will be impaired in their ability to support the troops and help ensure national security if required to perform and/or recognize same-sex marriages. By centering the free practices of believers opposed to same-sex relationships, these groups sought legal recognition for religious liberty as protecting the rights of private citizens and organizations to enforce normative beliefs about sexualized and gendered behaviors.

See table 2 for centrality scores. sections.paper_id: Espinoza-Kulick - 2020 - A MULTIMETHOD APPROACH TO FRAMING DISPUTES SAME-SEX MARRIAGE ON TRIAL IN OBERGEFELL V. HODGES
paragraphs: This case study of the amicus briefs and Court discourse in the Obergefell v. Hodges case demonstrates how embedded, opposed contention over legal and social definitions of marriage were used to frame dominant, oppositional, and polarized topics. These framing processes were identified through network analysis of anchoring and bridging themes (Freeman 1977;Ghaziani and Baldassarri 2011;Stovel and Shaw 2012) among topics generated by LDA topic modeling (Chang and Blei 2009;DiMaggio et al. 2013;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013). By using this method, I showed shows how units of meaning take on shared and disparate positions in constructing multiple simultaneous narratives that legitimate sociopolitical action. Specifically, a frame's position as an anchor or bridge among opposing social actors indexed their organization into dominant, oppositional, and polarized roles in the larger conversation between groups.

In the case of same-sex marriage, among the body of discourse produced by SCOTUS, dominant anchors focused on constitutional interpretation to emphasize the stability of the state. At the same time, these topics were used to instantiate tension among amicus brief signers. Legal advocates debated over competing visions of gay and lesbian family formations as the foundation for contention over federal-judicial power, which primarily concerns the Court. Individual topics have varying relationships to one another in these discursive spaces, demonstrating the nested analysis of anchoring and bridging topics. In this case, opposing groups of amicus brief writers shared an understanding of families as protecting the rights of private individuals against a hostile public. This supports the notion that same-sex marriage simultaneously advances both progressive and conservative visions of citizenship (Kimport 2013;Weber 2015).

This approach also shows the intricacies of framing and one of the computational methods available to analyze these processes. This methodological strategy is epistemologically aligned with other multimethod approaches to studying framing dynamics, as well as the broader sociological work calling for qualitative applications of computational procedures (Evans and Aveces 2016;Lazer and Radford 2017;Nelson 2017). The combination of computational and interpretive analyses helps to illuminate a multiplicity of meanings associated with framing disputes, as well as distilling structural patterns over a large swath of data (Ragin 1987). Structural topic modeling may be especially useful for extending these findings, while also examining document-level covariates (see Roberts, Stewart, Tingley, Lucas, Leder-Luis, Gadarian, Albertson, and Rand 2014). For instance, when examining legal mobilization, characteristics of the organizations and individuals submitting amicus briefs may help to distinguish between how different actors frame closely disputed legal concepts like "liberty" and "marriage". In such a study, the multimethod logic presented here could be deployed to compare distinct movement dynamics, such as the discourse surrounding samesex marriage with that of abortion or voting rights. Future research could extend this method by examining how social movements influence the development of social and legal concepts, for instance by examining text networks that can track topics over time that are associated with changing vocabularies (see Rule, Cointet and Bearman 2015). This approach might examine case law over time that has shaped the formation of variously framed concepts like same-sex marriage, gay marriage, domestic partnerships, civil unions, and other relationship recognition structures.
